text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
End-of-Treatment / Transition to Surveillance Visit

Patient: Renata Okonkwo
Age: 40 years
Diagnosis: Stage IIB (T2 N1 M0) Triple-Negative Invasive Ductal Carcinoma, Left Breast, Grade 3
Final Pathologic Stage (post-neoadjuvant): ypT1b ypN1 M0, RCB Class II
BRCA1 Status: Germline pathogenic variant confirmed (c.5074del, p.Cys1692ValfsTer17, heterozygous)
ECOG Performance Status: 1
Attending: [Attending Oncologist]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Ms. Okonkwo presents today for an end-of-treatment oncology follow-up visit. She has now completed all planned systemic therapy for her triple-negative breast cancer, including neoadjuvant pembro plus dose-dense AC followed by weekly PTX, definitive bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy with axillary dissection, adjuvant Xeloda for 8 cycles, and a total of 17 doses of pembro spanning both the neoadjuvant and adjuvant settings. She is here to review her overall treatment course, address active toxicities, and plan the transition to survivorship surveillance.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Okonkwo is a 40-year-old Nigerian-American premenopausal woman (at time of diagnosis; now post-RRSO surgically menopausal) with a confirmed BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant who was diagnosed approximately 14-15 months ago with Stage IIB triple-negative invasive ductal carcinoma of the left breast (3.2 cm, grade 3, ER/PR/HER2-negative, Ki-67 85%, basal-like phenotype, CPS 18 by PD-L1 immunohistochemistry). She had one biopsy-confirmed left axillary lymph node at presentation.

She received neoadjuvant therapy per the KEYNOTE-522 backbone: pembro 200 mg IV q3 weeks combined with dose-dense doxo and cyclo (ddAC) every 2 weeks for 4 cycles, followed by pacli 80 mg/m2 IV weekly for 12 weeks (with a 20% dose reduction to 64 mg/m2 from approximately week 7 onward due to Grade 2 peripheral neuropathy) plus pembro every 3 weeks for an additional 4 doses. Her neoadjuvant course was complicated by pembro-induced immune thyroiditis (initial thyrotoxic phase followed by persistent hypothyroidism), for which she required pembro to be held for one cycle and was started on levothyroxine, which she continues. She also experienced grade 2-3 hand-foot syndrome during the Xeloda phase and grade 2 peripheral neuropathy during the Taxol phase.

She underwent bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy with left axillary lymph node dissection plus right prophylactic mastectomy. Surgical pathology confirmed residual invasive ductal carcinoma (ypT1b) with 2 of 14 axillary lymph nodes involved (ypN1), classifying her as RCB Class II -- not a pathologic complete response. Receptor status on residual tumor remained triple-negative.

Adjuvant therapy consisted of cape 1000 mg/m2 BID (subsequently dose-reduced to 750 mg/m2 BID after Grade 2-3 hand-foot syndrome in cycles 2-3) on days 1-14 of each 21-day cycle for 8 cycles, combined with pembro 200 mg IV every 3 weeks to complete a total of 1 year of checkpoint inhibitor therapy. During the final planned dose of Keytruda (dose 17 of 17), she developed Grade 2 immune-related pneumonitis with bilateral lower lobe ground-glass opacities on CT. Keytruda was held (and ultimately not restarted, as this was the final planned dose). She was treated with prednisone 1 mg/kg/day, tapered over 6 weeks, with excellent clinical and radiographic response. She is now approximately 4-6 weeks beyond completing the prednisone taper, and her respiratory symptoms have fully resolved.

Most recently, her stage 2 breast reconstruction was completed by plastic surgery, with exchange of bilateral tissue expanders for silicone implants. She is recovering well from that procedure.

Today she presents reporting that she feels "cautiously hopeful" and is beginning to mentally process the transition out of active treatment. She denies new symptoms of concern. She has some residual tingling in her fingertips, which is improving. She reports mild fatigue, which she attributes to recovery from both the reconstruction surgery and the prolonged course of treatment. She denies dyspnea, cough, chest pain, or any respiratory symptoms. She denies new bone pain, headaches, or neurologic changes.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Mild fatigue (improved from prior visits). Denies fever, chills, or unintentional weight change.
Respiratory: No dyspnea at rest or with moderate exertion. Occasional mild dry cough, much improved from prior. No pleuritic chest pain.
Cardiovascular: No palpitations (resolved after immune thyroiditis treatment). No chest pain. No lower extremity edema.
Gastrointestinal: No nausea, vomiting. Bowel movements regular. Mild intermittent loose stools, attributed to residual effect of Xeloda; improved.
Musculoskeletal: Residual bilateral fingertip tingling and mild numbness, present but improving over the past 4-6 weeks. Bilateral foot tingling as well, Grade 1. No joint pain at present.
Skin: Bilateral palmar and plantar skin has largely healed from hand-foot syndrome. Mild residual dryness. Continues urea-based moisturizer.
Endocrine/Metabolic: Hot flashes and night sweats consistent with surgical menopause following RRSO (undergone since last visit). Mood changes noted but manageable.
Neuropsychiatric: Ongoing psychological adjustment to cancer diagnosis, BRCA1 carrier status, and surgical menopause at a young age. Engaged with oncology social work. Reports anxiety related to cancer recurrence (fear of recurrence). No depression requiring pharmacologic intervention at this time.
Genitourinary: Vaginal dryness. No dysuria.
Lymphatic: No new lymphedema. Left arm circumference measurements checked -- stable, no significant discrepancy from right.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (Established, previously documented)
- Stage IIB TNBC left breast, diagnosed age 39 -- as above
- Keytruda-induced autoimmune hypothyroidism (chronic, well-controlled on levothyroxine)
- pembro-induced pneumonitis (Grade 2, resolved; prednisone taper now complete)
- Palmar-plantar erythrodysesthesia (cape-related, Grade 2-3, now largely resolved)
- Peripheral neuropathy (pacli-related, Grade 1, improving)
- BRCA1 germline pathogenic variant carrier (c.5074del)
- Status post bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy with left ALND and immediate bilateral tissue expander placement
- Status post bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (risk-reducing, laparoscopic)
- Status post stage 2 reconstruction: bilateral tissue expander exchange for silicone implants

Surgical history as above.
Family history: Mother diagnosed with breast cancer at age 42; maternal aunt with ovarian cancer.
Social history: High school biology teacher. G2P2. Premenopausal at diagnosis; now surgically menopausal. Non-smoker. No alcohol use documented. Strong family support system.
Allergies: No known drug allergies.
Medications:
  - Levothyroxine 75 mcg orally daily (autoimmune hypothyroidism)
  - Urea 40% cream to hands and feet BID (maintenance)
  - Prednisone taper: NOW COMPLETED
  - Venlafaxine to be discussed/initiated for vasomotor symptoms (see plan)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: BP 112/70 mmHg, HR 72 bpm (regular), RR 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, Temperature 98.3°F, Weight 67.2 kg (baseline ~68 kg; stable).

General: Well-appearing, well-groomed woman in no acute distress. Appears in good health overall.

Head and Neck: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpable. No thyromegaly.

Chest / Breast: Bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy scars well-healed, no erythema, no wound dehiscence. Bilateral silicone implants in place; symmetrical, without signs of infection, hematoma, or capsular contracture on initial inspection. No palpable chest wall nodules or skin thickening to suggest locoregional recurrence. No palpable axillary adenopathy bilaterally.

Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheezes, or rubs. Good air movement throughout. Respiratory exam completely normal -- significant improvement from pneumonitis episode.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, gallops, or rubs. No peripheral edema.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatomegaly or splenomegaly. Laparoscopic port sites from RRSO well-healed.

Extremities / Neurologic: Bilateral fingertip tingling on pinprick testing, diminished light touch in fingertips and toes compared to proximal extremities. Motor strength 5/5 throughout. Gait normal. No edema. Left arm: no lymphedema.

Skin: Palms and soles: residual mild dryness, no erythema, no active blistering. Hair: growing back (previously Grade 2 alopecia from AC; now approximately 2-3 cm regrowth).

---

LABORATORY AND DIAGNOSTIC RESULTS

Today's labs reviewed:
- CBC: WBC 6.1 K/uL, ANC 3,800/uL, Hgb 12.1 g/dL (mild anemia, stable), Plt 234 K/uL
- Comprehensive metabolic panel: Na 138, K 4.0, Cl 102, CO2 25, BUN 14, Cr 0.79, Glucose 92. AST 24 U/L, ALT 28 U/L, Alk phos 67 U/L, Total bili 0.5 mg/dL. All within normal limits. (LFTs completely normalized after prior Grade 1 elevation on Xeloda)
- Thyroid function: TSH 3.6 mIU/L (within normal range, target 0.5-4.0 on replacement therapy). Free T4 1.1 ng/dL (normal). Levothyroxine dose unchanged.
- Inflammatory markers: CRP 2.1 mg/L (normal). No signs of active inflammation consistent with pneumonitis resolution.
- Cortisol (AM, to evaluate post-steroid adrenal function given recent prolonged prednisone course): 14.2 mcg/dL -- adequate adrenal function, no evidence of adrenal suppression requiring further intervention.

Imaging reviewed at today's visit:
- Repeat chest CT (obtained approximately 4 weeks ago at time of pneumonitis follow-up): Complete resolution of the previously identified bilateral lower lobe ground-glass opacities. No new pulmonary nodules. No pleural effusion. Bilateral implants in expected position. No chest wall nodularity.
- No new cross-sectional imaging ordered today; last surveillance CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis obtained approximately 6 months post-surgery was clear (no evidence of metastatic disease).

Cardiac: LVEF was last assessed at 59% after 2 cycles of doxo during the neoadjuvant phase (baseline 62%). Given completion of treatment well beyond the AC phase, a follow-up echocardiogram will be ordered today at the 12-month post-treatment interval to establish a new post-treatment baseline.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. TRIPLE-NEGATIVE BREAST CANCER -- END OF PLANNED TREATMENT / TRANSITION TO SURVEILLANCE

Ms. Okonkwo has now completed all planned systemic therapy for her stage IIB TNBC. To summarize her treatment course:
   - Neoadjuvant: Keytruda + ddAC x4 cycles, then weekly Taxol (dose-reduced from cycle 7) + Keytruda x12 weeks/4 doses
   - Surgery: Bilateral nipple-sparing mastectomy, left ALND (ypT1b ypN1 M0, RCB Class II)
   - Adjuvant: cape x8 cycles (dose-reduced after Grade 2-3 HFS) + pembro x9 additional doses (17 total)
   - Total Keytruda exposure: 1 full year (17 doses), as planned per KEYNOTE-522
   - Reconstruction: Bilateral tissue expanders exchanged for silicone implants
   - Risk reduction: Risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy (pathology negative)

She did not achieve a pathologic complete response (RCB Class II, ypT1b ypN1), which places her in a group with modestly elevated recurrence risk, particularly in the first 2-3 years. However, she completed the adjuvant cape per CREATE-X protocol, which has been shown to improve outcomes in non-pCR TNBC. Additionally, completing a full year of Keytruda per KEYNOTE-522 is associated with improved event-free survival. We are cautiously optimistic.

I discussed with Renata today that there is no standard of care systemic therapy to offer at this time beyond surveillance. We reviewed the OlympiA data for adjuvant ola in BRCA1/2 carriers with high-risk HER2-negative breast cancer; she has completed cape and Keytruda, and the OlympiA eligibility window (initiated within 12 weeks of chemotherapy/surgery completion) has passed given her sequential adjuvant regimen. We discussed this openly, and she understands. If disease recurs, PARP inhibitor-based therapy (ola monotherapy or platinum-based regimens) would be strongly considered at that time given her BRCA1 status.

Surveillance plan:
   - Oncology visits: Every 3 months for the first year post-treatment completion, then every 4-6 months in years 2-3, then annually thereafter
   - CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: Every 6-12 months for the first 3 years given non-pCR, TNBC, and BRCA1 status (institutional surveillance protocol)
   - Annual chest wall/skin MRI of bilateral reconstructed breasts (right mastectomy site and left chest wall) beginning at 1 year post-surgery, as bilateral mastectomy does not eliminate small risk of skin/chest wall recurrence, particularly relevant for BRCA1 carriers
   - No routine CA 27.29 or CA 15-3 tumor marker monitoring per ASCO guidelines; not recommended for TNBC surveillance
   - Annual clinical breast exam (chest wall and axilla)
   - Echocardiogram ordered today (post-anthracycline surveillance)

2. pembro-INDUCED PNEUMONITIS -- RESOLVED

Grade 2 immune pneumonitis developed during dose 17 (final dose) of Keytruda. She completed prednisone 1 mg/kg/day with slow 6-week taper, now fully completed approximately 4-6 weeks ago. CT chest confirms complete radiographic resolution. She is clinically asymptomatic from a pulmonary standpoint. SpO2 98% on room air today. Pulmonology saw her during the acute event and has discharged her from active follow-up. No further corticosteroids indicated. Will monitor for any signs of recurrent pulmonary or other immune-related symptoms given the potential for persistent immune dysregulation following checkpoint inhibition. She should be counseled to report any new respiratory symptoms promptly.

3. IMMUNE-MEDIATED HYPOTHYROIDISM -- WELL-CONTROLLED

Chronic sequela of pembro-induced thyroiditis. Currently on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH 3.6 mIU/L today -- within target range. No dose adjustment needed. Will continue thyroid function monitoring every 6 months going forward, or sooner if symptomatic. Patient is aware she will likely require lifelong thyroid hormone replacement.

4. PERIPHERAL NEUROPATHY -- GRADE 1, IMPROVING

Residual PTX-induced peripheral neuropathy affecting bilateral fingertips and toes, now Grade 1 and improving from the Grade 2 nadir during the weekly PTX phase. No motor deficits. Reassured that PTX-induced neuropathy typically continues to improve over 6-12 months following completion of treatment, though a small degree of residual deficit may persist. No pharmacologic intervention warranted at Grade 1. Will continue to monitor. If progresses or causes functional limitation, can consider duloxetine (noting she is already on venlafaxine -- see below).

5. SURGICAL MENOPAUSE / MENOPAUSAL SYMPTOMS FOLLOWING RRSO

Ms. Okonkwo underwent risk-reducing bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy given BRCA1 carrier status, inducing surgical menopause. She is experiencing moderate vasomotor symptoms (hot flashes 6-8/day, night sweats) and vaginal dryness. Hormone replacement therapy is not appropriate given her history of TNBC. Initiating venlafaxine (Effexor) 37.5 mg daily with planned uptitration to 75 mg daily at 2 weeks, which has good evidence for vasomotor symptom management in breast cancer patients. Referred to menopause clinic for comprehensive management. Vaginal moisturizers (non-hormonal, e.g., Replens) recommended. Discussed pelvic floor physical therapy referral. Bone density (DEXA scan) ordered given surgical menopause at age 40 and prior corticosteroid exposure; will assess need for bisphosphonate therapy at follow-up.

6. BRCA1 CARRIER STATUS -- ONGOING MANAGEMENT

Risk-reducing BSO completed (pathology negative bilaterally). Breast surveillance ongoing per above plan (annual chest wall MRI). No further cancer-risk-reducing surgical options relevant to her present anatomy (bilateral mastectomy complete, BSO complete). Genetic counseling remains active; patient is engaged in discussions about first-degree relatives. Her two children (presumably prepubertal/adolescent given patient age) should be counseled regarding BRCA1 testing when they reach adulthood (recommended at age 18-25). Patient is in contact with the hereditary cancer program.

7. BILATERAL BREAST RECONSTRUCTION -- COMPLETED

Stage 2 reconstruction performed by plastic surgery with exchange of tissue expanders for silicone implants. Excellent cosmetic result. No signs of infection or early capsular contracture. Follow-up with plastic surgery for ongoing reconstruction care. Patient satisfied with the result and reports this has contributed positively to her body image and psychological recovery.

8. PSYCHOSOCIAL / SURVIVORSHIP

Ms. Okonkwo carries a significant emotional burden: young-onset TNBC diagnosis, BRCA1 carrier status, surgical menopause at 40, prolonged and demanding treatment course, ongoing fears of recurrence, and impact on her role as a mother and teacher. She is engaged with oncology social work and has been evaluated by psychology. I had an extended conversation today about survivorship, the meaning of NED (no evidence of disease), and realistic expectations around recurrence risk. She was tearful at points but expressed relief at having completed treatment. I validated her experience and reinforced that surveillance is active and evidence-based.

Referral to a TNBC survivorship support group placed. Will provide contact information for the Young Survival Coalition (YSC) given her age at diagnosis.

Return to work: She has expressed desire to return to full-time teaching. We discussed a gradual return, monitoring for fatigue. If fatigue remains problematic, referral to integrative oncology for assessment.

FERTILITY: Previously banked embryos prior to neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Fertility status is now moot given bilateral oophorectomy; she was counseled regarding this prior to RRSO and understood its implications.

SUMMARY

Renata Okonkwo is a 40-year-old BRCA1-positive woman who has completed all planned therapy for Stage IIB TNBC and is currently with no evidence of disease. She has navigated an intensive, year-long treatment course with significant toxicities including immune thyroiditis, peripheral neuropathy, hand-foot syndrome, and pneumonitis, all of which have been managed and have largely resolved or improved. She transitions today to an active surveillance program. Long-term goals include close oncologic surveillance for recurrence, management of surgical menopause, ongoing BRCA1-related risk management, and psychosocial support as she returns to her life as a survivor.

Next visit: In 3 months.

Orders placed today: Echocardiogram (post-treatment cardiac assessment), DEXA scan (baseline bone density, post-surgical menopause and steroid exposure), venlafaxine 37.5 mg Rx, Replens vaginal moisturizer recommendation, pelvic floor PT referral, YSC survivorship group contact information provided.

--- Electronically signed by Attending Oncologist ---
